source_intake_record.raw_question_text: Consider a $2-\text{cm}$ thick plastic scintillator directly coupled to the surface of a photomultiplier with a gain of $10^{6}$. A $10-\text{GeV}$ particle beam is incident on the scintillator as shown in Fig. 4.8(a). Suppose one could detect a signal on the anode of as little as $10^{-12}$ coulomb. If the beam particle is a neutron, estimate what is the smallest laboratory angle that it could scatter elastically from a proton in the scintillator and still be detected?

multi_step: To solve, one must connect detection threshold on the photomultiplier anode to a minimum scintillation signal, then to recoil-proton energy, then to neutron-proton elastic scattering kinematics.
The problem combines detector-response reasoning with scattering-angle estimation rather than a single direct lookup.
Answer is a derived numerical angle, suggesting chained physical calculations.
verifiability: answer_type is short_text and the provided answer is a specific numeric value with units.
The task is a quantitative physics estimation problem with a determinate target quantity, so solution steps can be checked against known formulas.

candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: Consider a $2-\text{cm}$ thick plastic scintillator directly coupled to the surface of a photomultiplier with a gain of $10^{6}$. A $10-\text{GeV}$ particle beam is incident on the scintillator as shown in Fig. 4.8(a). Suppose one could detect a signal on the anode of as little as $10^{-12}$ coulomb. If the beam particle is a neutron, estimate what is the smallest laboratory angle that it could scatter elastically from a proton in the scintillator and still be detected?
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL object representation rather than a file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain:
Figure $4.8(\text{b})$ shows a neutron scatters by a small angle $\theta$ in the laboratory frame. Its momentum is changed by an amount $p \theta$ normal to the direction of motion. This is the momentum of the recoiling nucleus. Then the kinetic energy acquired by it is
$$
\frac{p^{2} \theta^{2}}{2 m}
$$
where $m$ is the mass of the recoiling nucleus. As an energy loss of 4 MeV corresponds to $3.2 \times 10^{-10}\text{C}$ of anode charge, the detection threshold of $10^{-12}\text{C}$ implies that recoil energy as little as 12.5 keV can be detected. Hence the smallest laboratory scattering angle $\theta_{\text{min}}$ that can be detected is given by
$$
\theta_{\text{min}}^{2}=\frac{2 m_{p}}{p_{n}^{2}} \times 12.5 \times 10^{3}=\frac{2 \times 10^{9}}{\left(10^{10}\right)^{2}} \times 12.5 \times 10^{3}=2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{rad}^{2}
$$
i.e.
$$
\theta_{\text{min}}=5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{rad}
$$
assuming the recoiling nucleus is a proton.

$$

where $m$ is the mass of the recoiling nucleus. As an energy loss of 4 MeV corresponds to $3.2 \times 10^{-10}\text{C}$ of anode charge, the detection threshold of $10^{-12}\text{C}$ implies that recoil energy as little as 12.5 keV can be detected. Hence the smallest laboratory scattering angle $\theta_{\text{min}}$ that can be detected is given by
$$
\theta_{\text{min}}^{2}=\frac{2 m_{p}}{p_{n}^{2}} \times 12.5 \times 10^{3}=\frac{2 \times 10^{9}}{\left(10^{10}\right)^{2}} \times 12.5 \times 10^{3}=2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{rad}^{2}

assuming the recoiling nucleus is a proton.
